title: The Anschluss — Annexation of Austria, March 1938
subtitle: Germany's bloodless absorption of its southern neighbour
author: VeteransLedger Archive
summary: On 12 March 1938, German troops marched into Austria, completing the Anschluss ('joining') that had been forbidden by the Treaty of Versailles. Austria ceased to exist as an independent state and was incorporated into the German Reich as the 'Ostmark'.

body.text: The Anschluss — the political union of Germany and Austria — had been Hitler's dream since his youth. Born in Austria, he regarded the two German-speaking states as a single nation divided by history. The Treaty of Versailles had explicitly forbidden Austro-German union.

By February 1938, Hitler had pressured Austrian Chancellor Kurt Schuschnigg into appointing Nazis to key cabinet posts. When Schuschnigg announced a plebiscite on Austrian independence to be held on 13 March, Hitler presented an ultimatum demanding his resignation and replacement by Nazi Arthur Seyss-Inquart. Schuschnigg complied and resigned.

German troops crossed the Austrian border at dawn on 12 March 1938. They encountered no resistance — many Austrians greeted them with cheering crowds and Nazi salutes. Hitler himself drove through his birthplace of Braunau am Inn and received a triumphant reception in Vienna. A plebiscite held on 10 April returned 99.73% approval — a figure influenced by intimidation and the exclusion of Jews and political opponents.

Britain and France protested diplomatically but took no action. Mussolini, who had previously threatened military action to protect Austrian independence, had since aligned with Germany and offered his support. The League of Nations proved impotent. The annexation demonstrated that the Western powers would not oppose Hitler's expansion within what he defined as the German ethnic sphere.

archival_note: Austrian diplomatic and police records from 1938 are held in the Österreichisches Staatsarchiv (Vienna), Archiv der Republik series. German Foreign Ministry documents on the Anschluss are in the Auswärtiges Amt records, partly available in the Documents on German Foreign Policy (DGFP) Series D, Volume I.

sources.ref: Österreichisches Staatsarchiv, Vienna. Archiv der Republik — Bundeskanzleramt: Anschluss documentation, February–April 1938.
Brook-Shepherd, Gordon. The Austrians: A Thousand-Year Odyssey. HarperCollins, 1996.
sources.note: Covers the political crisis leading to the Anschluss in depth.

sources.ref: Bukey, Evan Burr. Hitler's Austria: Popular Sentiment in the Nazi Era. University of North Carolina Press, 2000.
sources.note: Analyses Austrian popular attitudes using local administrative records.

sources.ref: Gedye, G.E.R. Fallen Bastions: The Central European Tragedy. Gollancz, 1939.
sources.note: Eyewitness account by a journalist in Vienna during the annexation.
